era_context: Late 15th century: Portuguese maritime expansion accelerating; Ottoman consolidation in the eastern Mediterranean; Italian Renaissance at its height; Ming China under the Hongwu and Yongle successors; Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl; the Iberian Reconquista in its final phase.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Interior Southeast Asia (limited European contact; local chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Bartolomeu Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope
events.description: Portuguese explorer Bartolomeu Dias, commissioned by King John II, successfully rounded the southern tip of Africa (Cape of Good Hope) in 1488, proving that a sea route to India was possible. Dias sailed around the cape and continued eastward along the African coast before turning back. This voyage was a watershed moment in European exploration, demonstrating that the Indian Ocean was accessible by sea and opening the path for subsequent Portuguese expansion and the eventual arrival of Vasco da Gama in India.

sources.name: Pêro de Alenquer, contemporary Portuguese chronicler
Portuguese royal records and navigational charts

events.certainty_note: The voyage is well-documented in contemporary Portuguese sources and has been extensively studied by modern historians.

events.title: Maximilian I elected Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: Maximilian I was elected Holy Roman Emperor in 1488, following the death of his father Frederick III. Maximilian's election marked a significant shift in the power and prestige of the Habsburg dynasty, which would dominate Central European politics for centuries. His reign was characterized by military campaigns, dynastic marriages, and efforts to centralize imperial authority. Maximilian was also known as a patron of the arts and a military innovator.

sources.name: Imperial election records and contemporary chronicles
Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Holy Roman Empire)

events.certainty_note: Maximilian's election is well-documented in imperial records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Charles V's election as Holy Roman Emperor, Maximilian's grandson)

events.title: Battle of Sauchieburn: James III of Scotland defeated and killed
events.description: King James III of Scotland was defeated and killed at the Battle of Sauchieburn on June 11, 1488, during a rebellion led by Scottish nobles and his own son, the future James IV. The battle resulted from long-standing tensions between James III and the Scottish nobility over his military failures, his choice of favorites, and his perceived weakness. James III's death marked a significant turning point in Scottish politics and led to the accession of James IV, who would prove to be a more capable and popular ruler.
key_figures: James IV of Scotland (rebel leader, future king)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of Scotland)

events.certainty_note: The battle and James III's death are well-documented in Scottish historical records.

events.title: James IV accedes to the Scottish throne
events.description: Following the death of James III at the Battle of Sauchieburn, his son James IV ascended to the Scottish throne in 1488. James IV was a capable and energetic ruler who worked to consolidate royal authority, improve Scotland's military capabilities, and enhance its international standing. His reign marked a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing in Scotland, and he became known as a patron of the arts and a military innovator.

sources.name: Scottish royal records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: James IV's accession is well-documented in Scottish historical records.
cross_references: 1488-003 (Battle of Sauchieburn and James III's death)
1513-001 (Battle of Flodden and James IV's death)

events.title: Inquisition intensifies in Spain under Isabella and Ferdinand
events.description: The Spanish Inquisition, established in 1478 and operating under the authority of Isabella and Ferdinand, intensified its activities in 1488. The Inquisition targeted Jews, Muslims, and Christian heretics, conducting trials, confiscations, and executions. By 1488, the Inquisition had become a powerful instrument of religious and political control, and its activities would accelerate in the following years, culminating in the expulsion of Jews from Spain in 1492.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus (multiple modern histories of the Spanish Inquisition)

events.certainty_note: The general intensification of Inquisition activities is well-documented; specific events in 1488 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Bartolomeu Dias's voyage is sometimes overstated in popular history as a 'discovery' of the Cape of Good Hope; Arab and Indian traders had long been aware of the southern African coast, and Dias's achievement was specifically in proving the feasibility of a European sea route. The Battle of Sauchieburn is sometimes portrayed as a clear-cut victory for James IV; modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of the political situation and the role of various factions. The intensification of the Spanish Inquisition in 1488 is sometimes presented as a sudden escalation; modern scholarship suggests a more gradual process of institutional development and increasing power.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1488 is rich for Europe (particularly Portugal, Spain, Scotland, and the Holy Roman Empire) due to extensive diplomatic archives, royal records, and contemporary chronicles. Asian sources (Ming China, Ottoman Empire) are also well-preserved. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is less well-attested. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, and the Pacific remain poorly documented for this year.

graph_edges.note: Dias's voyage proved the feasibility of the sea route to India, enabling Vasco da Gama's expedition
James III's death at Sauchieburn led directly to James IV's accession
Maximilian I's election began the Habsburg dominance of the Holy Roman Empire
Intensification of the Inquisition in 1488 contributed to the expulsion of Jews from Spain in 1492
